title: Exploratory Analysis
text: The exploratory analysis shows that the cleaned dataset contains 72,476 job postings and uses ANALYSIS_SALARY_INDUSTRY_IMPUTED as the main salary variable for salary-based analysis. After industry-median imputation, 39,244 postings have usable salary values and 33,232 postings still have missing salary information. Salary missingness remains important and should be considered when interpreting salary-based results. The remote-work distribution contains a large unknown category, so later remote comparisons should focus on clearly labeled remote, hybrid, and not-remote postings. The geographic and industry distributions show where job-posting activity is concentrated and help guide later salary, AI, and regional analysis.

title: Salary & Compensation Trends
section: How do salaries differ across AI vs. non-AI careers?
text:
How do salaries differ across AI vs. non-AI careers?

import us

salary_distribution = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN a.job_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'AI-related'
        ELSE 'Non-AI'
    END AS career_type,
    CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE) AS salary
FROM job_postings j
LEFT JOIN ai_job_ids a
    ON j.id = a.job_id
WHERE
    j.salary IS NOT NULL
    AND CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE) > 0
""")

salary_distribution_pd = salary_distribution.toPandas()

import plotly.express as px

fig = px.box(
    salary_distribution_pd,
    x="career_type",
    y="salary",
    color="career_type",
    points=False,
    title="Salary Distribution: AI vs Non-AI Careers"
)

fig.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    showlegend=False,
    yaxis_title="Salary ($)",
    xaxis_title=""
)

fig.show()

title: Salary & Compensation Trends
section: What regions offer the highest-paying jobs in AI-related and traditional careers?
text:
What regions offer the highest-paying jobs in AI-related and traditional careers?

state_salary = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    l.state_name,
    CASE
        WHEN a.job_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'AI-related'
        ELSE 'Non-AI'
    END AS career_type,
    ROUND(AVG(CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE)),0) AS avg_salary,
    COUNT(*) AS postings
FROM job_postings j
LEFT JOIN ai_job_ids a
    ON j.id = a.job_id
LEFT JOIN locations l
    ON j.location_id = l.location_id
WHERE
    j.salary IS NOT NULL
    AND CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE) > 0
    AND l.state_name IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY
    l.state_name,
    CASE
        WHEN a.job_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'AI-related'
        ELSE 'Non-AI'
    END
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 30
""")

state_salary_pd = state_salary.toPandas()

from us import states

state_salary_pd["state_code"] = (
    state_salary_pd["state_name"]
    .apply(lambda x: states.lookup(x).abbr if states.lookup(x) else None)
)

import plotly.express as px

ai_map = state_salary_pd[
    state_salary_pd["career_type"] == "AI-related"
]

fig = px.choropleth(
    ai_map,
    locations="state_code",
    locationmode="USA-states",
    color="avg_salary",
    scope="usa",
    hover_name="state_name",
    title="Average AI Salary by State"
)

fig.show()

title: Salary & Compensation Trends
section: Are remote jobs better paying than in-office roles?
text:
Are remote jobs better paying than in-office roles?

remote_salary = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    COALESCE(remote_type_name,'Unknown') AS remote_type,
    ROUND(AVG(CAST(salary AS DOUBLE)),0) AS avg_salary,
    COUNT(*) AS postings
FROM job_postings
WHERE
    salary IS NOT NULL
    AND CAST(salary AS DOUBLE) > 0
GROUP BY remote_type_name
ORDER BY avg_salary DESC
""")

remote_salary_pd = remote_salary.toPandas()

fig = px.bar(
    remote_salary_pd,
    x="remote_type",
    y="avg_salary",
    color="remote_type",
    text="avg_salary",
    title="Average Salary by Remote Work Arrangement"
)

fig.update_traces(
    texttemplate="$%{text:,.0f}",
    textposition="outside"
)

fig.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    showlegend=False
)

fig.show()

title: Salary & Compensation Trends
section: What industries saw the biggest wage growth in 2024?
text:
What industries saw the biggest wage growth in 2024?

industry_salary_monthly = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    i.naics_2022_6_name AS industry,
    MONTH(j.posted_date) AS month,
    ROUND(
        AVG(CAST(j.salary AS DOUBLE)),
        0
    ) AS avg_salary
FROM job_postings j
LEFT JOIN industries i
    ON j.industry_id = i.industry_id
WHERE
    YEAR(j.posted_date) = 2024
    AND j.salary IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY
    i.naics_2022_6_name,
    MONTH(j.posted_date)
""")

industry_salary_monthly_pd = industry_salary_monthly.toPandas()

top_industries = (
    industry_salary_monthly_pd
    .groupby("industry")["avg_salary"]
    .mean()
    .nlargest(12)
    .index
)

plot_df = industry_salary_monthly_pd[
    industry_salary_monthly_pd["industry"].isin(top_industries)
]

fig = px.line(
    plot_df,
    x="month",
    y="avg_salary",
    color="industry",
    markers=True,
    title="Monthly Salary Trends by Industry (2024)"
)

fig.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    xaxis_title="Month",
    yaxis_title="Average Salary"
)

fig.show()
